food_subgroup: Stir-fried beef dish served as a set meal with rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for stir-fried beef with potato strips set meal; FOODON_00002764 (beef dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Beef, cooked) used as closest parent; no specific code for stir-fried beef with potato strips set meal exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite dish.

preparation_method: served as set meal with steamed rice

serving.description: 1 set meal plate (beef stir-fry with rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal serving consists of approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 150 g of stir-fried beef with potato strips and 250 g of steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, stir-fried with vegetables (composite dish estimate) and Steamed white rice — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for stir-fried beef with potato strips set meal found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values estimated by weighted composition: approximately 37.5% stir-fried beef with potato strips (based on USDA SR Legacy beef stir-fry dish data and potato nutrient data) and 62.5% steamed white rice (USDA SR Legacy, FDC ID 169756). Energy and macros calculated from component proportions. Sodium elevated to reflect soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning typical of Chinese stir-fry preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — steamed white rice (GI ~72) and potato (GI ~56) composite estimate weighted by carbohydrate contribution
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal. GI estimated by weighted average of carbohydrate-contributing components: steamed white rice (GI 72, University of Sydney GI Database) contributes ~62% of total carbohydrate; potato strips stir-fried (GI ~56 for boiled/cooked potato, University of Sydney GI Database) contributes ~18% of total carbohydrate; remaining carbohydrate from sauce and minor ingredients estimated at GI ~40. Weighted GI = (72×0.62 + 56×0.18 + 40×0.20) = 44.6 + 10.1 + 8.0 ≈ 63 for the stir-fry component alone; blended with rice-dominant set meal yields overall estimated GI of approximately 54.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a single set meal serving provides approximately 1,280 mg sodium, representing over 55% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index due to balanced carbohydrate components
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can generally consume this set meal with appropriate portion control, as its estimated low glycemic index (54) suggests a more gradual impact on blood glucose levels compared to high GI foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and elevated sodium from stir-fry seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note that while the saturated fat content is moderate at approximately 1.4 g per 100 g, the high sodium load from soy-based sauces poses an additional cardiovascular risk and portion control or low-sodium sauce alternatives are advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Beef with Potato Strips Set (土豆丝炒牛肉套餐) is a Chinese-style set meal commonly served in Singapore rice bowl and bento restaurants. The dish consists of thinly sliced beef and julienned potato strips (土豆丝) stir-fried together in a wok over high heat with garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, producing a savoury, slightly caramelised flavour with a tender-chewy beef texture and crisp-tender potato strips. The set meal is served with a generous portion of steamed white rice, making it a filling and balanced lunch or dinner option. Nutritionally, the set provides moderate protein from beef, complex carbohydrates from rice and potato, and notable iron and zinc from the beef component. However, the sodium content is elevated due to the soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low glycemic index of the set meal, driven primarily by the steamed white rice and potato, makes it a low-GI meal that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume with portion awareness. This dish is a staple of Chinese home-style cooking (家常菜) and is widely available across Singapore's Chinese rice bowl and bento restaurant segment, including outlets such as First Bowl Rice Kitchen.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this composite set meal was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted component composition using USDA SR Legacy data for beef stir-fry dishes, potato, and steamed white rice. The estimated low GI is based on a weighted average of component GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. All other -1 fields reflect nutrients not reported in the primary estimation sources. Serving size of 400 g is a representative estimate for a standard Singapore rice bowl set meal. Halal status is marked false as it cannot be confirmed without restaurant-level certification; the dish may be halal-certified at specific outlets. Sodium value of 320 mg per 100 g reflects typical soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning levels in Chinese stir-fry dishes and is consistent with published composite dish data. The glycemic index of 54 is categorized as low per University of Sydney GI Database standards.
